dependencies: Contract manufacturers for assembly of servers and devices
Single or limited sources for some technical infrastructure components
Industry supply capacity for AI accelerators (GPUs and TPUs) is highly competitive
risks: Supply shortages, price increases, quality issues, longer lead times
Raw material and component availability constraints
Geopolitical tensions and armed conflicts affecting supply
Natural disasters, pandemics, climate change effects
Sanctions, export controls, trade disputes and barriers
Forced labor concerns and sustainability sourcing requirements
Concentration risk - some components available from only one or limited sources
Competitors may claim supply or capacity first for AI accelerators

regulatory_risks: Competition laws and antitrust enforcement (US DOJ, state AGs, European Commission)
Digital Markets Act (EU) - data sharing, consent requirements, interoperability
AI regulations (EU AI Act, US Executive Order on AI, state-level AI laws)
Data privacy and security laws (GDPR, UK GDPR, US state privacy laws, COPPA)
Copyright and intellectual property laws (EU Copyright Directive, news publisher rights)
Content moderation laws (Digital Services Act, state laws in Florida and Texas)
Consumer protection laws (EU New Deal for Consumers)
Environmental sustainability and climate change regulations
Tax law changes and OECD minimum global tax rate (15%)
Digital services taxes and revenue-based taxes in various jurisdictions
Google Play billing policies and fee regulations (South Korea, Japan, India, UK)
Data localization laws and data transfer restrictions
Age-appropriate design codes and children's privacy laws

sanction_risks: Sanctions and export controls may prevent operations in certain jurisdictions
Geopolitical tensions and armed conflicts (Middle East, Ukraine) may increase cyber attack risks
